text:
That sounds like exactly where it's supposed to feel awful, which is a strange kind of good news. Frustration usually means you've pushed past the part your brain could fake and hit the part it actually has to rewire. The smooth, easy stretch is behind you not because you've failed but because you've arrived at the real work. Nobody feels frustrated by things they've already mastered or things they haven't truly started; that ugly middle is the only place worth being stuck.

Remember that the confusion you're feeling right now is not evidence that you can't do this. It's evidence that you're doing it. The version of you from a month ago couldn't even be confused about this; you didn't know enough to be confused yet. That's real progress, even though it feels like spinning your wheels. Competence almost never arrives as a clean upward line. It comes in these maddening plateaus where nothing seems to move, and then one day the thing that baffled you for weeks just quietly makes sense, and you can't even reconstruct why it was ever hard.

So be a little gentler with yourself. You'd never expect a friend to be instantly good at something genuinely difficult, so try not to hold yourself to a standard you'd find absurd for anyone else. Step away when you need to, sleep on it, and let your brain do its quiet background work. The frustration is the cost of admission for learning something that's actually worth knowing, and the fact that you care enough to be annoyed tells me you're not going to quit. Keep going. I'm proud of you for staying in the hard part, and I genuinely can't wait to hear about the moment it clicks.
